title: The Nervous System and Singing: Why Vocal Expression Heals Trauma
excerpt: Discover how singing supports nervous system regulation and trauma healing through vocal expression and somatic connection.
body:
Introduction: The Body Keeps the Score—and the Voice Speaks It

When trauma lodges in the body, traditional talk therapy often falls short. Bessel van der Kolk’s seminal work The Body Keeps the Score highlights how trauma is stored not just in memories but in the nervous system itself—manifesting as tension, dysregulation, and sometimes, silence. But what if healing could come through the voice, a direct somatic pathway to the nervous system?

Singing is more than an art form or a hobby. It’s a powerful somatic practice that engages the vagus nerve, regulates the autonomic nervous system (ANS), and helps release trapped trauma. This article explores how vocal expression heals trauma through nervous system regulation, drawing on the research of Stephen Porges, Peter Levine, Deb Dana, and others.

The Nervous System 101: Trauma’s Lingering Footprint

Understanding why singing helps requires a quick primer on the nervous system. Trauma hijacks the ANS, especially the balance between the sympathetic (fight/flight) and parasympathetic (rest/digest) branches. When trauma triggers fight, flight, or freeze responses, the nervous system can get stuck in survival mode.

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory explains how the vagus nerve, the main parasympathetic nerve, influences our sense of safety and social engagement. The vagus nerve connects the brainstem to the voice box (larynx), heart, and lungs, mediating how we regulate emotions and communicate through vocal tone and facial expressions.

How Trauma Affects Vocal Expression

Trauma often suppresses the voice—literally silencing survivors. This isn’t just metaphorical. Chronic stress and hypervigilance tighten the muscles around the larynx, restrict breath, and reduce vocal range. Pat Ogden, founder of Sensorimotor Psychotherapy, notes that trauma survivors frequently experience difficulty vocalizing because their nervous system blocks expressive pathways.

In essence, trauma can trap the voice, making authentic expression nearly impossible.

Why Singing Heals: The Science Behind Vocal Expression

Singing naturally engages the vagus nerve, stimulating the parasympathetic nervous system and promoting regulation. Here’s how:

  • Vagal Tone Improvement: Stephen Porges’ research shows that vocalization—especially prolonged exhalations in singing—increases vagal tone, which boosts social engagement and emotional resilience.
  • Breath Regulation: Singing requires controlled breathing, which directly calms the nervous system by activating the diaphragm and slowing the heart rate (Deb Dana).
  • Emotional Release: Peter Levine’s somatic approach highlights that expressive vocalization helps discharge the energy trapped in trauma, breaking the freeze response and restoring flow.
  • Social Connection: Singing with others triggers endorphin release, creating a sense of safety and belonging—key for trauma recovery (Gabor Maté).

Research Supporting Singing for Trauma Recovery

Numerous studies support singing as a trauma-informed somatic practice:

  • A 2016 study published in Evidence-Based Complementary and Alternative Medicine found group singing improved mood and reduced stress hormones in trauma survivors.
  • Research from the University of Gothenburg demonstrated that singing enhances heart rate variability (HRV), a marker of vagal tone and autonomic regulation.
  • Francine Shapiro, the founder of EMDR, acknowledged the importance of integrating body-based practices like vocalization to complement cognitive trauma treatments.

Practical Ways to Use Singing in Your Trauma Healing

You don’t need to be a professional singer to benefit. Here are accessible ways to use vocal expression therapeutically:

  • Start with simple humming: Humming activates the vagus nerve gently and can be calming when nervous system dysregulation is intense.
  • Use sustained vowel sounds: Prolong “ah,” “oh,” or “ee” to engage breath control and vagal tone.
  • Explore call-and-response singing: Singing back and forth with a trusted person or recording fosters social engagement and safety.
  • Express emotions aloud: Allow your voice to express anger, grief, or joy through improvised singing or toning.
  • Incorporate breath-focused singing exercises: Slow inhalations followed by long exhalations through song help regulate the nervous system.

Tips for Staying Safe and Grounded

Trauma healing is not linear. Vocal expression can stir up strong emotions or sensations. To stay safe:

  • Practice in a safe environment where you feel comfortable.
  • Use grounding techniques before and after singing, like feeling your feet on the floor or mindful breathing.
  • Start slow and honor your limits—don’t push your voice or emotions too hard too fast.
  • Consider support from a somatic therapist if you experience overwhelming distress.

Integrating Singing with Other Somatic Modalities

Vocal expression works best when integrated with broader somatic healing practices:

  • Somatic Experiencing (Peter Levine): Use vocalization to release trauma energy alongside body-awareness exercises.
  • Polyvagal-Informed Therapy (Deb Dana): Combine singing with nervous system mapping and pacing techniques.
  • Sensorimotor Psychotherapy (Pat Ogden): Pair vocal exercises with mindful movement and touch.
  • Internal Family Systems (Richard Schwartz): Use singing to access and soothe different internal parts or subpersonalities.

Conclusion: Your Voice as a Pathway to Healing

Trauma doesn’t just live in your memories; it lives in your body and your voice. Singing reconnects you to your body, breath, and nervous system in a profoundly healing way. The science is clear: vocal expression activates the vagus nerve, regulates your autonomic nervous system, and unlocks trapped trauma energy.

By reclaiming your voice, you reclaim your sense of safety and agency in the world. As Gabor Maté reminds us, trauma recovery is about connection—to ourselves and others—and singing can be a powerful bridge for that connection.

"Singing is a direct line to the vagus nerve and the nervous system’s regulation centers. It’s a somatic doorway to healing trauma that words alone can’t open." — The Oracle Lover

If you’ve felt stuck in therapy or silenced by trauma, consider adding singing as a somatic tool. Your voice is waiting to speak your healing story.

References

  • van der Kolk, Bessel. The Body Keeps the Score. Penguin Books, 2015.
  • Porges, Stephen. The Polyvagal Theory. Norton, 2011.
  • Levine, Peter A. Waking the Tiger. North Atlantic Books, 1997.
  • Dana, Deb. The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy. Norton, 2018.
  • Ogden, Pat, et al. Sensorimotor Psychotherapy. Norton, 2006.
  • Maté, Gabor. When the Body Says No. Wiley, 2003.
  • Siegel, Daniel J. The Developing Mind. Guilford, 1999.
  • Schapiro, Francine. EMDR Institute resources, 2020.

faqs.q: How does singing directly impact the nervous system?
faqs.a: Singing stimulates the vagus nerve through controlled breathing and vocalization, enhancing parasympathetic activity which calms and regulates the nervous system.

faqs.q: Can anyone use singing to heal trauma, even without musical training?
faqs.a: Absolutely. Healing singing focuses on vocal expression and breath, not performance quality. Simple humming or vowel sounds can be effective.

faqs.q: What if singing triggers strong emotional responses?
faqs.a: It’s common for vocal expression to bring up emotions. Use grounding techniques and proceed at your own pace. Support from a somatic therapist can help if needed.

faqs.q: How does singing complement traditional trauma therapy?
faqs.a: Singing engages the body and nervous system directly, complementing cognitive approaches by releasing stored trauma energy and improving regulation.

affiliateProducts.description: A foundational guide to understanding and applying nervous system regulation in trauma therapy.

affiliateProducts.title: Waking the Tiger by Peter Levine
affiliateProducts.description: Explores the somatic approach to trauma healing, emphasizing bodily release of trauma energy.

affiliateProducts.title: The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk
affiliateProducts.description: Seminal book on how trauma affects the body and how somatic practices aid recovery.

metaTitle: How Singing Heals Trauma via Nervous System Regulation
metaDescription: Explore how vocal expression and singing stimulate the nervous system to heal trauma and promote somatic regulation.
